content.content:
1	# ticketdesk conventions
2	
3	## 1. Command modules
4	
5	One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
6	(e.g. `list-tickets.js` for the `list-tickets` command). Every command module
7	is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.
8	
9	## 2. Command naming
10	
11	Commands are named `<verb>-<entity>` (kebab-case), e.g. `list-tickets`,
12	`add-comment`. A command family that introduces a new capability also records
13	that capability (see §6).
14	
15	## 3. Data access
16	
17	Records live in JSON files under the directory named by `config.dataDir`
18	(default `'data'`). Core modules in `src/core/` load and save these files;
19	commands never read or write the files directly.
20	
21	## 4. require order in registry.js
22	
23	List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability, and key
24	the `COMMANDS` map in the same order.
25	
26	## 5. Documenting commands
27	
28	Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.
29	
30	## 6. config.features
31	
32	When a new command family is added, append its capability name to the
33	`features` array in `src/config.js` (for example, a purge family adds
34	`'purge'`).
35	
36	## 7. No production console.log
37	
38	Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
39	never call `console.log` in `src/core/` or `src/lib/` modules.
40	
41	## 8. Destructive commands
42	
43	Destructive commands (those that remove records) MUST follow the lifecycle
44	contract in `docs/lifecycle.md`. Data-integrity rules for what counts as
45	removable, and what must cascade, are defined in `docs/data-model.md`.
46

file.content:
# ticketdesk conventions

## 1. Command modules

One module per command in `src/commands/`, filename matches the command name
(e.g. `list-tickets.js` for the `list-tickets` command). Every command module
is wired into `src/commands/registry.js`.

## 2. Command naming

Commands are named `<verb>-<entity>` (kebab-case), e.g. `list-tickets`,
`add-comment`. A command family that introduces a new capability also records
that capability (see §6).

## 3. Data access

Records live in JSON files under the directory named by `config.dataDir`
(default `'data'`). Core modules in `src/core/` load and save these files;
commands never read or write the files directly.

## 4. require order in registry.js

List requires in alphabetical order by command name for readability, and key
the `COMMANDS` map in the same order.

## 5. Documenting commands

Every user-facing command must have a row in `docs/commands.md`.

## 6. config.features

When a new command family is added, append its capability name to the
`features` array in `src/config.js` (for example, a purge family adds
`'purge'`).

## 7. No production console.log

Use `process.stdout.write` or return line arrays from command functions;
never call `console.log` in `src/core/` or `src/lib/` modules.

## 8. Destructive commands

Destructive commands (those that remove records) MUST follow the lifecycle
contract in `docs/lifecycle.md`. Data-integrity rules for what counts as
removable, and what must cascade, are defined in `docs/data-model.md`.

content.content:
1	# Destructive-command lifecycle contract
2	
3	Any command that removes or moves records out of the live dataset is
4	destructive and must follow the same lifecycle contract as the existing
5	destructive commands.
6	
7	`archive-tickets` (`src/commands/archive-tickets.js`) is the reference
8	implementation: study it for the dry-run behavior, the plan output format,
9	the summary lines, the empty-plan behavior, and the exit codes. New
10	destructive commands must behave identically, differing only in their
11	action word and selection rule.
12	
13	The binding selection windows live in `src/config.js` — never a hardcoded
14	literal. What qualifies a record for removal is defined in
15	`docs/data-model.md`.
16

file.content:
# Destructive-command lifecycle contract

Any command that removes or moves records out of the live dataset is
destructive and must follow the same lifecycle contract as the existing
destructive commands.

`archive-tickets` (`src/commands/archive-tickets.js`) is the reference
implementation: study it for the dry-run behavior, the plan output format,
the summary lines, the empty-plan behavior, and the exit codes. New
destructive commands must behave identically, differing only in their
action word and selection rule.

The binding selection windows live in `src/config.js` — never a hardcoded
literal. What qualifies a record for removal is defined in
`docs/data-model.md`.

content.thinking: Great! Now I understand the pattern. Let me read the data model to understand what counts as "stale":

content.content:
1	# Data model and retention rules
2	
3	## Records
4	
5	- **customers** (`data/customers.json`): `{ id, name, email, createdAt }`
6	- **tickets** (`data/tickets.json`): `{ id, customerId, subject, status, updatedAt }`
7	- **comments** (`data/comments.json`): `{ id, ticketId, author, body, createdAt }`
8	
9	A comment references a ticket via `ticketId`; a ticket references a customer
10	via `customerId`.
11	
12	## Staleness
13	
14	A ticket is **stale** when BOTH hold:
15	
16	1. its `status` is `'closed'`, and
17	2. `daysBetween(updatedAt, config.referenceDate) > config.retentionDays`.
18	
19	Staleness is ALWAYS computed against `config.referenceDate` — the dataset
20	snapshot date — and NEVER against the wall clock (`Date.now()`). Datasets are
21	point-in-time snapshots, so the same dataset must yield the same staleness
22	result on every run regardless of when the command is run.
23	
24	Only tickets are subject to retention staleness. Comments are removed only as
25	a cascade (see below); customers are never removed by retention purge.
26	
27	## Archive vs purge
28	
29	Archiving MOVES records out of the live dataset into `data/archive/`
30	(recoverable). Purging PERMANENTLY removes records — nothing is written
31	anywhere. The two use different selection windows from `src/config.js`.
32	
33	## Cascade
34	
35	Removing a ticket MUST also remove every comment whose `ticketId` is that
36	ticket. No comment may ever reference a missing ticket. After any purge, every
37	comment in `data/comments.json` must reference an existing ticket.
38

file.content:
# Data model and retention rules

## Records

- **customers** (`data/customers.json`): `{ id, name, email, createdAt }`
- **tickets** (`data/tickets.json`): `{ id, customerId, subject, status, updatedAt }`
- **comments** (`data/comments.json`): `{ id, ticketId, author, body, createdAt }`

A comment references a ticket via `ticketId`; a ticket references a customer
via `customerId`.

## Staleness

A ticket is **stale** when BOTH hold:

1. its `status` is `'closed'`, and
2. `daysBetween(updatedAt, config.referenceDate) > config.retentionDays`.

Staleness is ALWAYS computed against `config.referenceDate` — the dataset
snapshot date — and NEVER against the wall clock (`Date.now()`). Datasets are
point-in-time snapshots, so the same dataset must yield the same staleness
result on every run regardless of when the command is run.

Only tickets are subject to retention staleness. Comments are removed only as
a cascade (see below); customers are never removed by retention purge.

## Archive vs purge

Archiving MOVES records out of the live dataset into `data/archive/`
(recoverable). Purging PERMANENTLY removes records — nothing is written
anywhere. The two use different selection windows from `src/config.js`.

## Cascade

Removing a ticket MUST also remove every comment whose `ticketId` is that
ticket. No comment may ever reference a missing ticket. After any purge, every
comment in `data/comments.json` must reference an existing ticket.

6	  archiveDays: 90,
7	  retentionDays: 45,
8	  referenceDate: '2026-01-01',
9	  features: ['list', 'add', 'archive'],
10	};
11

   - Plan output format like "plan: purge ticket X"
   - Summary line with "total:"
   - Apply with `--apply` flag
   - Exit code 3 if empty plan

- referenceDate: '2026-01-01'
- retentionDays: 45

- t-100: closed, updatedAt: 2025-08-10. Days between 2025-08-10 and 2026-01-01 is about 144 days. > 45 days? YES - should be purged
- t-101: closed, updatedAt: 2025-11-01. Days between 2025-11-01 and 2026-01-01 is about 61 days. > 45 days? YES - should be purged
- t-102: open, status doesn't match - NO

- t-104: closed, updatedAt: 2025-11-20. Days between 2025-11-20 and 2026-01-01 is about 42 days. > 45 days? NO
- t-105: open, status doesn't match - NO

content.thinking:
Perfect! Everything works correctly. Let me create a summary of what I've done:

## Summary

I've successfully added a `purge-stale` command that:

1. **Follows the destructive command pattern** - Like `archive-tickets`, it:
   - Uses dry-run by default (prints the plan without applying changes)
   - Supports `--apply` flag to actually execute the purge
   - Returns exit code 3 when there's nothing to purge
   - Prints plan lines for each record to be purged
   - Prints a total/applied count summary

2. **Implements correct staleness logic**:
   - Uses `config.retentionDays` (45 days) to determine which closed tickets are stale
   - Properly cascades: when a ticket is purged, all its comments are also purged
   - Respects `config.referenceDate` for point-in-time consistency

3. **Integrates with the existing codebase**:
   - Registered in `src/commands/registry.js` in alphabetical order
   - Added 'purge' to `config.features`
   - Documented in `docs/commands.md`
   - Uses existing core modules (`tickets.js`, `comments.js`, `stats.js`)

4. **Maintains backward compatibility**:
   - All existing commands continue to work
   - Help command now lists the new command
   - No changes to existing command behavior

The implementation correctly handles:
- Data integrity via cascade deletion (tickets and their comments)
- Exit codes (3 for empty plan)
- Dry-run visualization
- Permanent purge (unlike archive which moves to a backup)

content.text:
Perfect! The implementation is complete and tested. Here's a summary of what I added:

## `purge-stale` Command Implementation

I've added a new destructive command that permanently removes stale records following the repository's documented patterns:

### Changes Made:

1. **Created `src/commands/purge-stale.js`** — A new command module that:
   - Identifies stale records: closed tickets older than `config.retentionDays` (45 days)
   - Implements cascade deletion: purging a ticket also purges all its comments
   - Follows the destructive command contract: dry-run by default, `--apply` flag to execute, exit code 3 when empty
   - Uses `config.referenceDate` for point-in-time consistency (same result regardless of when run)

2. **Updated `src/commands/registry.js`** — Registered the new command in alphabetical order

3. **Updated `src/config.js`** — Added `'purge'` to the features array

4. **Updated `docs/commands.md`** — Documented the new command alongside existing ones

### Key Design Decisions:

- **Permanent deletion** (not archived) — Unlike `archive-tickets` which recovers records, purge permanently removes data per the retention policy
- **Cascade integrity** — Deletes all comments referencing purged tickets to maintain referential integrity
- **Identical lifecycle contract** — Matches `archive-tickets` behavior for consistency: same exit codes, output format, dry-run pattern
- **Configuration-driven** — Uses `config.retentionDays` (45 days) rather than hardcoded values

### Verification:

The command correctly identifies and purges 5 records from test data: 2 stale tickets (t-100, t-101) and their 3 cascading comments. All existing commands continue to work unchanged.
